images.caption: Figure 1:TideGS enables city-scale 3DGS training on a single GPU by virtualizing the Gaussian parameter table across the SSD–CPU–GPU hierarchy and materializing only the trajectory-activated working set in VRAM.

Figure 2:TideGS pipeline.(a) Out-of-core hierarchy.The full scene is stored as SSD-resident blocks; CPU RAM maintains a warm cache and schedules prefetch; GPU VRAM acts as a working-set cache for rendering and backpropagation.(b) Trajectory-adaptive differential streaming.Consecutive block working sets𝒦t\mathcal{K}_{t}and𝒦t+1\mathcal{K}_{t+1}guide reuse, while TideGS selects capacity-bounded resident setsℛt\mathcal{R}_{t}andℛt+1\mathcal{R}_{t+1}, retains their overlap, streams only𝒮t+=ℛt+1∖ℛt\mathcal{S}_{t}^{+}=\mathcal{R}_{t+1}\setminus\mathcal{R}_{t}, and evicts𝒮t−=ℛt∖ℛt+1\mathcal{S}_{t}^{-}=\mathcal{R}_{t}\setminus\mathcal{R}_{t+1}while overlapping SSD/PCIe transfers with GPU compute.
①–⑤ indicate the corresponding cross-tier dataflow steps.

Figure 3:Block virtualization and two-stage visibility filtering.We (top-left) Morton-sort Gaussians and partition them into SSD-aligned blocks to preserve spatial locality,
(top-right) summarize each block with a bounding sphere,
and (bottom) perform CPU-side 6-plane frustum tests to select visible blocks for H2D staging.
Fine-grained Gaussian-level filtering is then executed on GPU within the retained blocks.

Figure 4:Quality scaling on MatrixCity.PSNR across different Gaussian counts (NN). “Standard”, “Large”, and “Billion” correspond to∼\sim25M,∼\sim102M, and∼\sim1.1B Gaussians, respectively. Native 3DGS and CLM encounter OOM at higher scales, whileTideGSenables billion-scale training on a single GPU and achieves the highest PSNR among evaluated methods (26.1 dB).
Figure 5:Convergence under different view orders.The curves compare randomized shuffling and trajectory ordering on Mip-NeRF 360bicycle, complementing the final metrics in Tab.7.

Figure 6:Dense initialization versus training-time densification onbicycle.Dense-initialized fixed-size training achieves comparable final quality to the standard densification setting on this outdoor scene.

Figure 7:Dense initialization versus training-time densification onbonsai.Dense-initialized fixed-size training achieves comparable final quality to the standard densification setting on this indoor scene.

Figure 8:Wall-clock time-to-convergence operating point.We compare TideGS with the distributed in-memory baseline, Grendel-GSZhaoet al.(2024), from the wall-clock perspective to contextualize the trade-off between hardware scale and single-GPU out-of-core capacity.

Figure 9:Iteration-wise convergence operating point.We compare TideGS with the distributed in-memory baseline, Grendel-GSZhaoet al.(2024), from the training-iteration perspective to separate optimization progress from per-iteration execution time.
